content.text: When you enable prompt caching, the system manages
(the model's internal map of how words connect) through a check-process-store cycle:
API Request → Check cache → If exists: use cached → If not: process full prompt → Store & respond
: System searches for existing cached version of your prompt
: Reuse saved attention states → no recalculation needed
: Process full prompt from scratch → build attention states → store for future
: Return result using cached or new attention states
Claude doesn't cache the text itself—it caches

, the internal representations of:
- How tokens relate to each other
- Contextual embeddings
- Positional information
- Semantic connections
No manual reset needed; clears automatically

: Part of static prefix, reused across all turns
-

: Lets model reuse earlier conversation context
-
: Conversation can continue in follow-ups without recalculating
Prompts smaller than these thresholds won't be cached—caching is only beneficial for longer prompts.
Check API response headers for cache usage:

: Cost at 125% of normal input price
-
: Cost at 10% of normal input price

Call 1 (write):  1,000 tokens × $3.75/MTok = $3.75
Call 2 (read):   1,000 tokens × $0.30/MTok = $0.30
Call 3 (read):   1,000 tokens × $0.30/MTok = $0.30
-----------------------------------------------
Total:           $4.35

Without caching:
3 calls × 1,000 tokens × $3.00/MTok = $9.00

Savings: 52% (approaches 90% with more reuse)
Slightly more expensive to set up (+25%), but dramatically cheaper to reuse (90% reduction)—optimal for

Turn 1: [System + User1] → Compute attention (100 tokens) → Response
Turn 2: [System + User1 + Assist1 + User2] → Compute attention (180 tokens) → Response
Turn 3: [System + User1 + Assist1 + User2 + Assist2 + User3] → Compute (260 tokens) → Response

Total computation: 100 + 180 + 260 = 540 token-computations

Turn 1: [System+User1] → Compute (100 tokens) → Cache → Response
Turn 2: [Cached System+User1 + Assist1 + User2] → Read cache (100) + Compute new (80) → Response
Turn 3: [Cached System+User1 + Assist1 + User2 + Assist2 + User3] → Read cache (100) + Compute new (80) → Response

Total computation: 100 + 80 + 80 = 260 token-computations (52% reduction)
System prompt and early messages are computed once, then reused across all turns.
Big data chunks reused across queries
Long prompts with repeated pattern examples
Common Pitfalls & How Caching Avoids Them
When working with how caching works, following established best practices ensures reliability, maintainability, and optimal performance.
Start simple and iterate — begin with the minimum viable implementation before adding complexity.
Test with representative inputs — ensure your test cases cover edge cases, not just the happy path.
Monitor performance — track latency, token usage, and error rates in production environments.
Version your prompts and configurations — treat prompt text like source code with proper version control.
Document your design decisions — record why you chose specific approaches for future maintainers.
Plan for failure — implement graceful degradation when the AI component is unavailable or returns unexpected results.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit what the system produces to catch quality drift over time.
These pitfalls frequently trip up developers working with how caching works. Knowing them in advance saves significant debugging time.
Assuming deterministic output — language models are probabilistic; the same input can produce different outputs across calls. Always validate the structure of responses.
Ignoring token limits — exceeding context window limits causes truncation or errors. Track token counts, especially in multi-turn conversations.
Over-engineering early — building complex infrastructure before validating basic functionality wastes effort. Get a working prototype first.
Hardcoding prompts in code — embed prompts in configuration files or databases so non-developers can iterate on them.
Skipping error handling — API calls fail due to rate limits, timeouts, or content policy violations. Always wrap calls in try/catch with appropriate retry logic.
As your usage of how caching works scales, these performance factors become increasingly important.
Latency budget — typical API latency ranges from 1-30 seconds depending on model size and output length. Design your UX around streaming to provide early feedback.
Concurrency limits — each API tier has request-per-minute (RPM) and token-per-minute (TPM) limits. Implement request queuing for burst traffic.
Prompt caching — use the cache_control breakpoint on stable, repeated prompt prefixes to reduce costs by up to 90% and latency by 85%.
Batching — for non-interactive workloads, the Message Batches API processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost reduction.
Model selection — use Claude Haiku for high-volume, low-complexity tasks and Claude Sonnet or Opus for complex reasoning where quality matters more than speed.
Follow these best practices when working with how caching works to maximize reliability and performance.
Start simple and iterate incrementally — validate your core approach before adding complexity.
Test with realistic inputs — use representative examples from your actual use case, not just toy examples.
Monitor in production — track latency, error rates, and output quality with structured logging and metrics.
Version control your configurations — treat prompts and settings like code with proper change tracking.
Handle errors gracefully — implement retry logic with exponential backoff for transient API errors.
Set appropriate timeouts — long-running requests should have explicit timeout limits to prevent hangs.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit produced content to catch quality drift as models are updated.
Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
These pitfalls catch most developers off guard. Learn from them upfront.
Treating LLM output as deterministic — outputs vary between runs. Always validate structure and handle unexpected formats.
Not accounting for token limits — conversations grow over time. Implement context truncation strategies before hitting limits.
Over-engineering before validation — build the simplest thing that works, then optimize based on real measurements.
Ignoring rate limits — API throttling causes failures under load. Implement request queuing and rate-limit-aware retry logic.
Hardcoding prompts in source code — separate prompt text from code to enable rapid iteration without deployments.
At scale, how caching works introduces specific performance challenges that require deliberate design decisions.
Latency: Claude Haiku responds in 1-5 seconds; Sonnet in 5-15 seconds; Opus in 15-30+ seconds. Choose the right model for your latency budget.
Throughput: Use async clients (AsyncAnthropic in Python, async/await in TypeScript) for concurrent requests rather than sequential blocking calls.
Cost optimization: Prompt caching reduces costs by 90% on repeated prefixes. The Message Batches API gives 50% discount for offline workloads.
Streaming: Use streaming responses to reduce perceived latency — users see output immediately rather than waiting for complete generation.
Connection pooling: Reuse HTTP connections with a persistent client instance rather than creating a new client per request.
Production deployments require attention to security boundaries and reliability engineering.
Never log API keys — rotate credentials immediately if they appear in logs, error messages, or version control history.
Validate and sanitize inputs — prompt injection attacks can exfiltrate data or override instructions if user inputs are embedded directly.
Implement circuit breakers — automatically stop sending requests when error rates exceed threshold to prevent cascading failures.
Audit all AI-generated actions — log every API call, input, and output with correlation IDs for debugging and compliance.

How Caching Works is a foundational aspect of building reliable Claude-powered applications. The patterns covered here — careful validation, incremental iteration, proper error handling, and performance-aware design — apply broadly across all Claude integrations. Master these fundamentals and you will be well-equipped to tackle more complex scenarios as your application evolves.
Cache Key Construction and Prefix Matching

Understanding exactly what constitutes a cache key is essential for reasoning about when hits will occur. The cache key is the exact token sequence from the beginning of the prompt up to and including the cache_control breakpoint. "Exact" means byte-identical: the same text in a different encoding, or with a different whitespace character, will produce a different key and a cache miss.

The token sequence includes system prompt tokens, tool definition tokens, and document content tokens in the order they are sent. If your system prompt is 2000 tokens and you place a breakpoint after it, those 2000 tokens form the prefix. Every subsequent request with the same 2000-token system prompt prefix will hit the cache, regardless of what follows in the messages array.

A common source of unexpected cache misses is dynamic content inserted into what you intend to be the static prefix. If your system prompt includes a timestamp, a request ID, or any per-request value before the breakpoint, every request gets a unique key and the cache never hits. Audit your prefix for any dynamically generated content and move it to after the breakpoint.

import anthropic
from datetime import datetime

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# WRONG: timestamp in the cached prefix causes cache miss every request
def bad_cached_request(user_message: str, large_document: str) -> str:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=[
            {
                "type": "text",
                "text": f"Current time: {datetime.now().isoformat()}\n"  # BREAKS CACHE
                        f"You are a document analyst.\n\n"
                        f"Document:\n{large_document}",
                "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}  # Cache miss every time!
            }
        ],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_message}]
    )
    return response.content[0].text

# CORRECT: static content in prefix, dynamic content after breakpoint
def good_cached_request(user_message: str, large_document: str,
                        request_id: str) -> str:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=[
            {
                "type": "text",
                # Static prefix - same every request
                "text": "You are a document analyst.\n\n"
                        f"Document:\n{large_document}",
                "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}  # Cache HIT on repeat requests
            },
            {
                "type": "text",
                # Dynamic content AFTER the breakpoint (not cached)
                "text": f"Request ID: {request_id}\nTime: {datetime.now().isoformat()}"
            }
        ],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_message}]
    )
    return response.content[0].text

You can place up to four cache_control breakpoints in a single request. Each breakpoint creates an independent cache entry for the prefix ending at that point. Multi-breakpoint strategies are useful when your prompt has multiple layers with different stability characteristics.

A typical three-layer prompt might consist of: a very stable system identity and instructions layer (changes monthly), a moderately stable tool definitions and examples layer (changes weekly), and a dynamic per-session document or conversation history layer (changes per user). Placing breakpoints at the end of each stable layer lets each layer's cache be independently managed—if you update your tool definitions, only the tool-layer cache needs to be invalidated, not the system prompt layer.
Layer 1 (most stable): core system identity, persona, safety instructions — breakpoint after this layer caches the most reusable tokens
Layer 2 (moderately stable): tool definitions, few-shot examples, domain knowledge — breakpoint creates a second cache entry for this addition
Layer 3 (least stable): session-specific context, conversation history — no cache_control needed here; it changes every request anyway
Breakpoints must appear in order from start to end; you cannot cache later content without caching earlier content
Each breakpoint incurs a small overhead on cache miss (the write cost); use only as many breakpoints as you have genuinely stable layers

Anthropic returns cache usage metrics in every response under response.usage. Monitoring these metrics lets you verify that caching is working as intended and calculate actual cost savings. The three key fields are: input_tokens (tokens billed at normal rate), cache_read_input_tokens (tokens read from cache at 0.1x rate), and cache_creation_input_tokens (tokens written to cache at 1.25x rate).

import anthropic
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

@dataclass
class CacheMetrics:
    total_requests: int = 0
    cache_hits: int = 0
    total_input_tokens: int = 0
    cache_read_tokens: int = 0
    cache_creation_tokens: int = 0

    def record(self, usage: anthropic.types.Usage) -> None:
        self.total_requests += 1
        self.total_input_tokens += usage.input_tokens
        cr = getattr(usage, "cache_read_input_tokens", 0) or 0
        cc = getattr(usage, "cache_creation_input_tokens", 0) or 0
        self.cache_read_tokens += cr
        self.cache_creation_tokens += cc
        if cr > 0:
            self.cache_hits += 1

    def hit_rate(self) -> float:
        if self.total_requests == 0:
            return 0.0
        return self.cache_hits / self.total_requests

    def estimated_savings_vs_no_cache(self, price_per_million: float = 15.0) -> float:
        """
        Estimate USD saved vs. paying full price for every token.
        Uses claude-3-5-sonnet-style pricing as an example.
        Cache read rate is ~0.1x, cache write is ~1.25x.
        """
        base_cost = (self.total_input_tokens / 1e6) * price_per_million
        actual_cost = (
            (self.total_input_tokens / 1e6) * price_per_million
            + (self.cache_read_tokens / 1e6) * (price_per_million * 0.1 - price_per_million)
            + (self.cache_creation_tokens / 1e6) * (price_per_million * 0.25)
        )
        return base_cost - actual_cost

metrics = CacheMetrics()

def make_request(system_with_cache: list, user_msg: str) -> str:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=system_with_cache,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_msg}]
    )
    metrics.record(response.usage)
    return response.content[0].text

# After several requests:
# print(f"Hit rate: {metrics.hit_rate():.1%}")
# print(f"Estimated savings: ${metrics.estimated_savings_vs_no_cache():.4f}")

Tool definitions—the list of tool objects you pass to the tools parameter—can be large, especially if you have many tools with detailed schemas. These definitions can and should be cached. To cache tool definitions, place them as the first items in the system list with a cache_control breakpoint, rather than passing them in the top-level tools parameter.

This works because the caching system treats the system prompt content as one stream of tokens, regardless of whether those tokens originally came from the system field or the tools field. Placing tool definitions in the system field with cache_control lets the cache key include them as part of the stable prefix. This is especially valuable when you have 10+ tools with long descriptions, which can easily exceed 5000 tokens of definition overhead per request.
Convert tool definitions to system prompt format when they are static and shared across many requests
Keep the same ordering of tool definitions in the system prompt; reordering changes the cache key
When a new tool is added, the entire post-addition prefix is a new cache key; plan tool additions as you would schema migrations
Test that tool-calling still works correctly when definitions are moved to the system prompt—the model handles both locations
Track cache_creation_input_tokens after adding new tools to confirm the new prefix was written to cache
